label: Evaluating Foundation Model Performance — Part A

questions.stem: Why is evaluating generative AI models more difficult than traditional ML models?

options.text: The output is non-deterministic, making evaluation more difficult because the same input can produce different outputs
Generative AI models cannot produce any measurable output
Traditional ML models never need evaluation
Generative AI always produces perfect output that needs no evaluation

explanation.correct: The output of generative AI models is non-deterministic, which makes evaluation more difficult. Unlike traditional ML where predictions are deterministic and can be directly compared to labels, gen AI evaluation requires task-specific metrics.

distractors.B: Gen AI produces text, images, and other outputs. The challenge is measuring quality, not producing output.
distractors.C: All ML models need evaluation. Traditional models are simply easier to evaluate with deterministic metrics.
distractors.D: Gen AI can hallucinate and produce errors. Evaluation is essential, not unnecessary.

questions.stem: What does the ROUGE metric evaluate?

options.text: Automatic summarization quality by evaluating how well generated summaries compare to reference summaries
Machine translation quality against human translations
Model inference speed in milliseconds
The cost efficiency of model deployment

explanation.correct: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) evaluates automatic summarization and machine translation by comparing how well the generated output matches reference text.

distractors.B: Translation quality evaluation is BLEU's primary purpose. ROUGE focuses on summarization tasks.
distractors.C: Inference speed is a latency metric, not a text quality evaluation metric.
distractors.D: Cost is a business metric. ROUGE measures text generation quality.

questions.stem: What does the BLEU metric evaluate?

options.text: The quality of machine-translated text by comparing it to human-generated translations
The quality of text summaries against reference summaries
Model training speed
GPU memory utilization during inference

explanation.correct: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) evaluates the quality of machine-translated text by comparing it against human-generated translations from one natural language to another.

distractors.B: Summarization evaluation is ROUGE's primary task. BLEU specifically handles translation quality.
distractors.C: Training speed is an operational metric, not a text quality measurement.
distractors.D: GPU memory is a hardware metric unrelated to translation quality evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the tradeoff when reducing model size to improve inference latency?

options.text: Smaller models load more quickly and reduce latency, but may decrease model performance and accuracy
Smaller models are always more accurate because they focus on essential features
Reducing model size has no effect on latency or performance
Smaller models require more compute resources to run

explanation.correct: Reducing model size improves inference latency (smaller model loads more quickly) but may decrease performance. This is a key tradeoff — you may trade model accuracy for speed or lower storage requirements.

distractors.B: Smaller models generally have lower accuracy because they have fewer parameters to capture patterns.
distractors.C: Size directly affects both latency (faster loading) and performance (fewer parameters). There is always an effect.
distractors.D: Smaller models require less compute. That's the reason for optimization — to reduce resource requirements.

questions.stem: What does BERTScore evaluate in Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: Semantic similarity between generated responses and human references — suitable for evaluating faithfulness and hallucinations in text generation
The number of parameters in the model
The cost of each API call
Network latency between the model and the user

explanation.correct: Amazon Bedrock's evaluation module uses BERTScore to automatically compare generated responses against human references by calculating semantic similarity. It's suitable for evaluating faithfulness and detecting hallucinations.

distractors.B: Parameter count is a model characteristic. BERTScore measures output quality against references.
distractors.C: API cost is a billing metric. BERTScore evaluates text quality.
distractors.D: Latency is a performance metric. BERTScore measures semantic similarity of generated content.

questions.stem: Which are established benchmarks for evaluating LLM performance? (Select THREE)

options.text: GLUE — General Language Understanding Evaluation with tasks like sentiment analysis and question answering
MMLU — Massive Multitask Language Understanding testing world knowledge across history, math, law, and more
HELM — Holistic Evaluation of Language Models combining metrics for summarization, Q&A, sentiment, and bias
GPU-BENCH — measuring graphics card performance
COST-EVAL — measuring deployment expenses
SPEED-TEST — measuring network bandwidth

explanation.correct: GLUE (language understanding tasks), MMLU (world knowledge across domains), and HELM (holistic metrics for multiple tasks) are established LLM benchmarks. Others include SuperGLUE and BIG-bench.

distractors.D: GPU-BENCH is not a real LLM benchmark. Hardware benchmarks are separate from model quality evaluation.
distractors.E: COST-EVAL is not a recognized benchmark. Cost is a business metric, not a model quality benchmark.
distractors.F: SPEED-TEST is not an LLM benchmark. Network speed is infrastructure, not model quality.

questions.stem: Which techniques can optimize LLM performance for deployment? (Select THREE)

options.text: Reducing model size to decrease inference latency
Making prompts more concise
Reducing the size and number of retrieved snippets in RAG
Always using the largest model available regardless of requirements
Increasing model parameters until latency is eliminated
Removing all inference parameters to simplify output

explanation.correct: Optimization techniques include reducing model size (faster loading), making concise prompts (less processing), and reducing retrieved snippet size/number in RAG. All may trade some accuracy for performance.

distractors.D: The largest model has highest latency. Optimization means finding the right size for your requirements.
distractors.E: More parameters increase latency. You cannot eliminate latency by adding parameters.
distractors.F: Inference parameters control output quality. Removing them doesn't optimize — it removes control.

questions.stem: How does RAG help improve foundation model performance in applications? (Select THREE)

options.text: Overcomes outdated knowledge by providing access to current external data at inference time
Helps avoid hallucinations by grounding responses in retrieved factual context
Improves relevance and accuracy of completions without retraining the model
Eliminates the need for any evaluation of model outputs
Automatically fixes complex math errors in all cases
Replaces the need for any model at all

explanation.correct: RAG overcomes outdated knowledge (accesses current data), avoids hallucinations (grounds responses in facts), and improves accuracy without retraining (adds context at inference). It avoids costly re-training.

distractors.D: RAG improves quality but evaluation is still needed to verify outputs meet objectives.
distractors.E: Models still struggle with complex math. RAG helps with factual grounding but doesn't solve all math errors.
distractors.F: RAG augments a model, not replaces it. The model still generates responses using retrieved context.

questions.stem: What questions should be asked when considering how your LLM will function in deployment? (Select THREE)

options.text: How fast does the model need to generate completions?
What compute budget is available?
Are you willing to trade model performance for improved inference speed or lower storage?
What color should the application interface be?
How many office buildings does the company have?
What programming language does the CEO prefer?

explanation.correct: Key deployment questions: completion speed requirements, compute budget availability, and willingness to trade performance for speed/storage. These determine optimization strategies and infrastructure choices.

distractors.D: Interface design is UX work, not model deployment planning.
distractors.E: Physical office count is irrelevant to model deployment decisions.
distractors.F: CEO language preference doesn't affect model infrastructure choices.

questions.stem: What are key components of the application stack for deploying generative AI? (Select THREE)

options.text: Infrastructure layer — compute, storage, and network to serve LLMs and host application components
Model layer — choosing LLMs and appropriate infrastructure for inference needs
User interface layer — website or REST API through which the application is consumed
Marketing layer — promoting the application to users
Legal layer — filing patents for each API endpoint
Physical layer — building new data centers for each model

explanation.correct: The application stack includes: infrastructure (compute/storage/network), model layer (LLM selection + inference infrastructure), and user interface (website/REST API). Security applies across all layers.

distractors.D: Marketing is a business function, not a component of the technical application stack.
distractors.E: Patent filing is legal, not a deployment stack component.
distractors.F: AWS provides data centers. You don't build new ones for each model deployment.

questions.scenario: A data science team needs to compare multiple LLMs on tasks that go beyond basic language understanding — including math, biology, physics, and software development.
questions.stem: Which benchmark specifically focuses on tasks beyond the capabilities of current language models?

options.text: BIG-bench (Beyond the Imitation Game Benchmark) — contains tasks in math, biology, physics, linguistics, reasoning, and software development
GLUE — tests basic sentiment analysis and question answering
BLEU — evaluates translation quality
ROUGE — evaluates summarization quality

explanation.correct: BIG-bench (Beyond the Imitation Game Benchmark) focuses on tasks beyond current model capabilities including math, biology, physics, bias, linguistics, reasoning, childhood development, and software development.

distractors.B: GLUE covers standard language understanding tasks. BIG-bench goes beyond these to more challenging domains.
distractors.C: BLEU is a translation evaluation metric, not a multi-domain capability benchmark.
distractors.D: ROUGE evaluates summarization. BIG-bench tests across many advanced domains.

questions.scenario: A company wants to evaluate whether an LLM has extensive world knowledge and problem-solving ability across subjects like history, mathematics, law, and computer science.
questions.stem: Which benchmark evaluates knowledge and problem-solving across these diverse academic domains?

options.text: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) — tests models on world knowledge across history, math, law, computer science, and more
HELM — focuses on model transparency
GLUE — tests basic language understanding
BERTScore — measures semantic similarity

explanation.correct: MMLU evaluates knowledge and problem-solving capabilities across diverse academic domains. Models must demonstrate extensive world knowledge beyond basic language understanding to perform well.

distractors.B: HELM improves transparency and guides model selection for tasks. MMLU specifically tests world knowledge breadth.
distractors.C: GLUE tests standard NLP tasks. MMLU tests broader academic knowledge across many subjects.
distractors.D: BERTScore measures text similarity. MMLU evaluates factual knowledge across domains.

questions.scenario: A company deploys an LLM to edge devices with limited memory and strict latency requirements of under 100ms. The full model exceeds memory limits and takes 500ms per completion.
questions.stem: What optimization approach addresses both memory and latency constraints?

options.text: Reduce model size — smaller models load more quickly (reducing latency) and consume less memory, though performance may decrease
Increase model parameters to improve speed
Add more edge devices to distribute the same large model
Switch from an LLM to a rule-based system for all tasks

explanation.correct: Reducing model size addresses both constraints: smaller model uses less memory (fits on device) and loads faster (meets latency requirement). The tradeoff is potential performance decrease.

distractors.B: More parameters means more memory and slower inference. This worsens both constraints.
distractors.C: The same large model still exceeds memory per device. Distribution doesn't solve per-device memory limits.
distractors.D: Rule-based systems can't handle LLM tasks. Optimization should maintain model capabilities while meeting constraints.

questions.scenario: A team wants to compare the quality of responses from multiple foundation models available in SageMaker JumpStart. They need automated metrics and also want human workers to assess response quality.
questions.stem: Which AWS service can create model evaluation jobs that support both automated metrics and human evaluation?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Clarify — creates model evaluation jobs to evaluate and compare LLMs from JumpStart, supporting both automated and human evaluation
Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor — tracks drift in production
Amazon SageMaker Feature Store — manages training features
Amazon SageMaker Pipelines — orchestrates workflow steps

explanation.correct: SageMaker Clarify can create model evaluation jobs to evaluate and compare quality metrics for text-based FMs from JumpStart. You can use human workers to compare responses and also specify models outside AWS.

distractors.B: Model Monitor tracks production drift, not comparative FM evaluation during selection.
distractors.C: Feature Store manages features for training, not model quality comparison.
distractors.D: Pipelines orchestrate workflows. Clarify specifically handles model evaluation jobs.

questions.scenario: A company deploys a generative AI chatbot and wants to determine whether it effectively meets their business objectives of increased productivity and user engagement.
questions.stem: What must they define and measure to evaluate business success?

options.text: Define specific business goals, determine metrics for success, and continuously measure, monitor, and review metrics to evaluate performance
Only measure the model's BLEU score
Count the number of model parameters as a success metric
Compare their model size to competitors' models

explanation.correct: You must define business goals (specific problem to solve), determine success metrics (productivity, engagement), provide supporting infrastructure, and continuously measure/monitor/review to evaluate performance.

distractors.B: BLEU measures translation quality. Business success requires broader metrics like productivity and engagement.
distractors.C: Parameter count is a model characteristic. Business success is measured by outcomes, not model size.
distractors.D: Competitor comparison is not a business evaluation metric. Focus on whether your objectives are being met.

questions.scenario: An architect is designing a RAG-based application and needs to understand the integration requirements.
questions.stem: What additional considerations does RAG add to model deployment? (Select TWO)

options.text: Requires additional configuration to connect the LLM to external data components
Needs an orchestration library to manage passing user input to the LLM and returning completions
Eliminates the need for any infrastructure
Removes the need for a user interface
Automatically handles all security without configuration

explanation.correct: RAG requires additional configuration to connect the LLM to external components, plus an orchestration library to manage the flow of user input → retrieval → LLM → completion return.

distractors.C: RAG adds infrastructure needs (vector databases, retrieval systems), not eliminates them.
distractors.D: Users still interact through a UI. RAG adds backend retrieval, not removes frontend interfaces.
distractors.E: Security must be explicitly configured. RAG adds security considerations for external data access.

questions.scenario: A research team is reviewing available LLM benchmarks to create a comprehensive evaluation plan.
questions.stem: What did SuperGLUE add beyond the original GLUE benchmark? (Select TWO)

options.text: Additional tasks such as multi-sentence reasoning
Reading comprehension tasks
Image generation evaluation
Audio quality scoring
Video analysis metrics

explanation.correct: SuperGLUE (introduced 2019) adds tasks beyond GLUE including multi-sentence reasoning and reading comprehension. Both GLUE and SuperGLUE have leaderboards for comparing models.

distractors.C: GLUE/SuperGLUE evaluate language understanding, not image generation.
distractors.D: Audio evaluation uses different metrics. GLUE/SuperGLUE are text-focused NLP benchmarks.
distractors.E: Video analysis is separate from language understanding benchmarks.

questions.scenario: A solutions architect is planning the infrastructure for a generative AI application. They need to consider security, storage, and user access.
questions.stem: What security and storage considerations apply to the gen AI application stack? (Select TWO)

options.text: Security must be ensured across the entire AI lifecycle — data preparation, training, and inferencing
Additional storage may be needed to collect user completions or feedback for fine-tuning and evaluation
Security is only needed at the user interface layer
No storage is needed beyond the model weights
Feedback collection is never useful for improving the model

explanation.correct: Security must span the entire AI lifecycle (prep, training, inference). Additional storage may be needed for collecting user completions/feedback for future fine-tuning, evaluation, or alignment.

distractors.C: Security applies at every layer, not just UI. Data isolation, model access, and network security all matter.
distractors.D: Storage is needed for user feedback, completions, RAG data, and monitoring — beyond just model weights.
distractors.E: User feedback is valuable for additional fine-tuning, alignment, and evaluation as the application matures.

questions.scenario: A team needs a benchmark that helps with model transparency and provides guidance on which model performs best for specific tasks like summarization, Q&A, and bias detection.
questions.stem: Which benchmark provides this holistic evaluation with task-specific guidance?

options.text: HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) — improves transparency and offers guidance on which model performs well for specific tasks
MMLU — tests world knowledge across academic subjects
BIG-bench — focuses on tasks beyond current model capabilities
BLEU — evaluates translation quality

explanation.correct: HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) improves model transparency and offers users guidance on which model performs well for specific tasks. It combines metrics for summarization, Q&A, sentiment, and bias.

distractors.B: MMLU tests knowledge breadth. HELM provides task-specific guidance and transparency across multiple evaluation dimensions.
distractors.C: BIG-bench tests challenging tasks. HELM provides holistic guidance across standard tasks with transparency focus.
distractors.D: BLEU is a single translation metric. HELM is a comprehensive benchmark combining multiple evaluation dimensions.

questions.scenario: A company wants to supplement automated metrics with human judgment when evaluating their LLM's responses. They need humans to compare outputs from multiple models side-by-side.
questions.stem: What approach can be used alongside automated benchmarks for LLM evaluation?

options.text: Use human workers to manually evaluate and compare model responses, including responses from SageMaker JumpStart models and models outside AWS
Human evaluation is never allowed for LLM assessment
Only automated metrics should be used because humans are always biased
Human workers can only evaluate traditional ML models, not LLMs

explanation.correct: You can use human workers to manually evaluate model responses. They can compare responses from SageMaker JumpStart models and also specify responses from models outside AWS for comprehensive evaluation.

distractors.B: Human evaluation is explicitly supported and recommended as a complement to automated metrics.
distractors.C: Human evaluation adds nuance that automated metrics miss. Both should be used together.
distractors.D: Human evaluation applies to LLMs specifically. SageMaker Clarify supports human workers for FM evaluation.
